items.prompt:
visit(node) processes node and then recursively calls visit(node.next) until node is None.
Which argument proves that every branch terminates for an acyclic finite list?
constraints: The list is finite and acyclic.
Every non-terminal call recurses exactly on node.next.

options.text: The number of nodes reachable from node is a non-negative integer that decreases by one.
The language runtime recognizes recursion and therefore stops it automatically.
The stored node values eventually become smaller.
A node-is-None base case exists somewhere in the code.

feedback.reason: Finite remaining-list size is well founded and strictly decreases when the call advances to node.next.
feedback.details: A termination proof needs a measure into a well-founded set, usually the non-negative integers, and a strict decrease on every recursive edge. For a finite acyclic list, let m(node) be the number of nodes reachable from node. The terminal node None has measure 0, and node.next has measure m(node) - 1 for every non-terminal node. Stored values are irrelevant. This proof also states its boundary: it fails for cyclic structures unless the state or algorithm prevents revisiting nodes.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.function_is_recursive: Runtimes allocate frames but do not prove progress. An unchanged node or a cycle would keep creating calls until stack failure.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.node_values_change: The traversal does not modify or order node values, so their magnitudes provide no progress measure. Termination depends on link structure.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.base_case_exists: A base case matters only if every valid recursive path reaches it. A cyclic list or an unchanged recursive argument could make it unreachable.

items.prompt: Order the steps for proving that every recursive branch reaches a base case.
constraints: The function's semantic result contract is already fixed.
The proof concerns termination of all legal recursive paths, not average behavior on selected examples.

elements.text: State the legal recursive states and all recursive transitions.
Map each legal state to a value in a well-founded set such as the non-negative integers.
Show that every recursive transition makes the measure strictly smaller.
Show that states at the measure's minimum are covered by terminal rules.

feedback.reason: A progress proof covers the whole state domain, every branch, and the terminal boundary of a well-founded measure.
feedback.details: The domain and transition list come first because an omitted branch can invalidate an otherwise correct argument. The measure must be defined for every legal state and cannot descend forever. Each recursive edge must strictly decrease it; non-increase is insufficient because equal-measure cycles remain possible. Finally, the minimum or terminal region of the measure must be handled without another recursive call. This structure applies to lengths, remaining nodes, numeric distances, tree heights, and divide-and-conquer ranges. Saying only “the input is smaller” omits the exact comparison and often hides an uncovered edge case.

items.prompt:
pal(l, r) compares s[l] and s[r], then calls pal(l + 1, r - 1) when they match.
Which terminal condition completes the progress proof for every finite inclusive interval?
constraints: The interval is inclusive.
The recursive transition changes both boundaries by one.
A zero- or one-character interval is already a palindrome.

options.text: Stop when l >= r.
Stop only when l == r.
Stop only when l > r.
Stop when r == 0.

feedback.reason: Removing both endpoints reaches size zero for even lengths and size one for odd lengths, so both l > r and l == r are terminal.
feedback.details: Use remaining length max(0, r - l + 1) as the progress measure. A non-terminal pair comparison requires l < r, and the child removes two positions. Odd lengths eventually have one center character, represented by l == r. Even lengths eventually have no character, represented by l > r. Both require no recursive work. This boundary generalizes to step sizes larger than one: terminal conditions must cover every residue class or overshoot state reachable under the transition, not only one visually convenient endpoint.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.l_equal_r_only: Even-length intervals move from two characters to l > r. Equality alone misses that reachable terminal state and can lead to invalid indexing.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.l_greater_r_only: Odd-length intervals reach l == r, where no pair remains. Recursing again would compare the center character with itself and then cross unnecessarily.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.r_equal_zero: The absolute right index is not a measure of remaining interval size. Many valid terminal and non-terminal intervals have unrelated r values.

items.prompt:
solve(lo, hi) handles the half-open range [lo, hi). When hi - lo > 1 it sets
mid = lo + (hi - lo) // 2 and recursively solves [lo, mid) and [mid, hi).
Which fact is required for termination?
constraints: lo and hi are integers with lo <= hi.
Recursion occurs only when hi - lo > 1.
The split uses floor integer division as shown.

options.text: Each child range has non-negative length strictly less than hi - lo.
The two child lengths add to the parent length.
mid is an integer.
The two recursive calls cancel one another's stack growth.

feedback.reason: For every non-terminal parent, lo < mid < hi makes both child lengths strictly smaller than the parent length.
feedback.details: Let m(lo, hi) = hi - lo. The terminal region is m <= 1. For m > 1, floor division places mid strictly between lo and hi, so m(lo, mid) and m(mid, hi) are both positive and both less than m(lo, hi). This proves termination on every branch. The fact that child ranges partition the parent is important for result correctness but is a separate obligation. The transfer rule for divide and conquer is to prove strict decrease independently for every generated child, not merely that the children collectively resemble the parent.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.combined_lengths_equal_parent: Coverage of the parent supports correctness, but it does not alone prove each branch is smaller. A split could include one unchanged child and one empty child.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.mid_is_integer: Integer type does not establish that mid lies strictly inside a non-terminal interval. The inequalities lo < mid < hi are the decisive property.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.two_calls_eventually_cancel: Calls do not cancel. Each branch must independently reach a terminal range under a well-founded measure.

items.prompt:
pairCount(i) counts complete adjacent pairs in a[i:] and recursively calls pairCount(i + 2).
It must work for arrays of either parity. Which terminal condition is required?
constraints: The array is immutable.
Each non-terminal call consumes exactly two positions.
Only complete pairs contribute to the result.

options.text: Stop when i + 1 >= len(a).
Stop only when i == len(a), because a base case handles only the empty suffix.
Stop only when i == len(a) - 1.
Use a base case only when the original array is empty.

feedback.reason: A two-position transition must stop for both zero and one remaining element; neither state contains a complete pair.
feedback.details: The progress measure is the number of elements remaining, len(a) - i. Each recursive call reduces it by two. Depending on the initial parity, the measure reaches 0 or 1. Both are terminal because the recursive operation requires two elements. An equality check for only the empty suffix reflects the false heuristic that base cases are exclusively empty inputs. The robust rule is to encode the operation's precondition: recurse only while at least one complete pair remains.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.i_equal_length: For an odd-length array, i advances from the last valid index to a state with one unpaired element; equality with len(a) is not guaranteed before a pair read would be invalid.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.i_equal_last_index: This covers the one-leftover case but misses the exact empty suffix reached from even lengths. The terminal region must cover both.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.array_empty_initially: Termination depends on the current suffix, not solely on the initial input. Non-empty arrays still reach a suffix with fewer than two elements.

items.prompt:
reduce(n) calls reduce(n // 2) for n > 1. A trace from n = 10 is 10 -> 5 -> 2 -> 1.
What proves progress?
constraints: n is a non-negative integer.
The base case handles n <= 1.
Division is floor integer division.

options.text: The non-negative measure n strictly decreases on every non-terminal call.
There is no progress because every frame has a parameter named n.
Progress is proved because stack depth increases with each call.
Any recursive expression containing division is guaranteed to terminate.

feedback.reason: The state schema remains one integer, while its value descends through a finite sequence to the terminal region.
feedback.details: A progress proof evaluates state values in each frame. For integer n > 1, floor division by two produces an integer satisfying 0 <= n // 2 < n. The base handles n <= 1, so no infinite descending chain is possible. Reusing the name n is normal because every call solves the same kind of subproblem. This distinction matters in traces: compare values under an explicit measure, not variable names, source-code line numbers, or stack depth.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.parameter_name_same: A shared parameter name is the state schema. The runtime values 10, 5, 2, and 1 are distinct and form a strict descent.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.stack_depth_increases: Stack depth increases even for nonterminating recursion. It is a consequence of calling, not a well-founded measure approaching a base.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.division_symbol: The concrete domain and rounding matter. For example, a transition that maps 1 back to 1 would not progress despite using division.

constraints: n is an integer.
Exactly one recursive branch executes in each non-terminal frame.

options.text: It cycles 1 -> 2 -> 1, so no well-founded measure decreases on every call.
It reaches 0 because every recursive function with a base case eventually uses it.
It terminates after two calls because n is halved on even frames.
The only issue is exponential time from having two condition branches.

feedback.reason: A present base case is irrelevant when reachable transitions form a cycle that never enters its terminal state.
feedback.details: Trace the actual state graph: f(1) calls f(2), and f(2) calls f(1). Any proposed scalar measure would have to be strictly smaller in both directions, which is impossible. The syntax contains n == 0, but reachability—not presence—determines termination. A repair must change the odd transition or broaden the contract so every reachable path descends under a valid measure. This is the decisive boundary between recursive code that merely has a base case and recursion with a complete progress proof.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.reaches_zero: The trace never reaches 0. The odd transition increases 1 to 2, and the even transition returns 2 to 1.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.terminates_after_two: Halving one branch is insufficient when another branch reverses that progress. All transitions in every reachable cycle must be considered.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.exponential_only: Only one recursive call executes per frame. The defect is nontermination through a cycle, not branching call-tree growth.

items.prompt:
Two functions use the same terminal rule n == 0.

A makes one call on n - 1.
B makes two calls on n - 1.

Which conclusion is valid?
constraints: n is a non-negative integer.
Both functions recurse only when n > 0.
B executes both recursive calls in every non-terminal frame.

options.text: Both terminate because n decreases on every branch, but B can do exponentially more total work.
Only A terminates because recursion with two calls is automatically nonterminating.
Both are linear because the progress measure has n possible values.
Both are exponential because both are recursive.

feedback.reason: Strict decrease bounds branch depth; branching factor separately determines total call-tree size.
feedback.details: For both functions, the measure n is a non-negative integer and every child has measure n - 1, so all branches terminate after at most n transitions. Function A has one child per frame and therefore a linear number of calls. Function B has two children per non-terminal frame, creating a full duplicated tree with exponential total calls. This item preserves the ownership boundary: the progress proof establishes termination but deliberately does not claim a time bound from depth alone. Complexity analysis must account for branching and overlap separately.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.only_a_terminates: Branch count affects total work, not whether an individual path reaches n == 0. Every path in B still decreases n by one.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.both_linear: A measure bounds path depth, not the number of nodes in a branching call tree. B duplicates descendants across branches.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.both_exponential: A forms a single chain with n non-terminal calls. Recursive syntax does not determine growth without the call structure.
